coredata.dc:title: Upcycling of exhausted reverse osmosis membranes into value-added pyrolysis products and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Lili
Veksha, Andrei
Mohamed Amrad, Muhammad Zahin Bin
Snyder, Shane Allen
Lisak, Grzegorz
coredata.dc:description: Polymeric reverse osmosis (RO) membranes are widely used worldwide for production of fresh water from various sources, primarily ocean desalination. However, with limited service life, exhausted RO membrane modules often end up as plastic wastes disposed of predominantly by landfilling. It is imperative to find a feasible way to upcycle end-of-life RO membrane modules into valuable products. In this paper, the feasibility of RO membrane recycling via pyrolysis and subsequent conversion of resulting char into carbon dots (CDs) through H2O2-assisted hydrothermal method was investigated. RO membrane module pyrolysis at 600 °C produced oil (28 wt%), non-condensable gas (17 wt%), and char (22 wt%). While oil and gas can serve as fuel and chemical feedstock due to rich hydrocarbon content, char was found a suitable precursor for the synthesis of functional CDs. The resulting CDs doped with N (4.8%) and S (1.8%) exhibited excellent water dispersibility, narrow size distribution of 1.3–6.8 nm, high stability, and strong blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 6.24%. CDs demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ in the range of 0–100 μM with the limit of detection of 2.97 μM and were capable of determining Fe3+ in real water samples (tap water and pond water).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymeric reverse osmosis (RO) membranes are widely used worldwide for production of fresh water from various sources, primarily ocean desalination. However, with limited service life, exhausted RO membrane modules often end up as plastic wastes disposed of predominantly by landfilling. It is imperative to find a feasible way to upcycle end-of-life RO membrane modules into valuable products. In this paper, the feasibility of RO membrane recycling via pyrolysis and subsequent conversion of resulting char into carbon dots (CDs) through H2O2-assisted hydrothermal method was investigated. RO membrane module pyrolysis at 600 °C produced oil (28 wt%), non-condensable gas (17 wt%), and char (22 wt%). While oil and gas can serve as fuel and chemical feedstock due to rich hydrocarbon content, char was found a suitable precursor for the synthesis of functional CDs. The resulting CDs doped with N (4.8%) and S (1.8%) exhibited excellent water dispersibility, narrow size distribution of 1.3–6.8 nm, high stability, and strong blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 6.24%. CDs demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ in the range of 0–100 μM with the limit of detection of 2.97 μM and were capable of determining Fe3+ in real water samples (tap water and pond water).

ce:section.ce:para: Polymeric membranes have been extensively used in pressure-driven membrane processes for water desalination, including nanofiltration, ultrafiltration, microfiltration, and reverse osmosis (RO). RO desalination as a prevalent technology contributed to 69% of the global water desalination capacity in 2018 (Jones et al., 2019). However, the increasing demand for water treatment has led to a rapid generation of abundant end-of-life RO membrane modules, owing to their limited life cycle of 5–7 years (Ziolkowska, 2015). A recent study reported that over 840,000 waste RO membrane modules (＞14,000 tonnes) were discarded globally every year (Landaburu-Aguirre et al., 2016), and most of them eventually end up in landfills, which is wasteful, unsustainable, and costly (Landaburu-Aguirre et al., 2016). As major components of RO membrane modules on the market are non-biodegradable polymer materials, such as polypropylene (PP), polyamide (PA), polysulfone (PSU), and polyester (Reinhard et al., 1986), the increasing disposal and accumulation of waste membranes would eventually lead to the long-lasting negative effects of plastic pollution. To minimize undesired environmental impacts and promote sustainable development, it is imperative to find a feasible way to upcycle exhausted RO membranes into valuable products instead of landfilling.

Among various approaches to convert waste into value-added products, such as electrochemical (Petersen et al., 2021), biochemical (Danso et al., 2019), and thermochemical routes (Nanda and Berruti, 2021), pyrolysis is one of the most commonly employed technique to achieve thermal decomposition of wide-ranging raw materials, such as biomass and waste plastics. This technique possesses outstanding flexibility in obtaining different proportions of liquid oil, gas, and char by tuning experimental parameters (e.g., gas flow rate, heating temperature, and heating rate) (Czajczyńska et al., 2017). Pyrolysis of wastes, especially non-biodegradable plastics, allows substantial reduction of waste volume and generation of valorized products that can be potentially used for energy and environmental applications (Miandad et al., 2016). Previous studies have preliminarily shown that pyrolysis can be applied for the treatment of RO membranes (Lawler et al., 2012; Pontié et al., 2017; Prince et al., 2011). Pontié et al. concluded from the TGA study that pyrolysis could lead to waste membrane mass reduction of 48.5% and thermal energy recovery of 86 MWh/year in a form of liquid and gaseous fuel from the globally discarded waste RO modules (i.e., 14,000 tonnes/year) (Pontié et al., 2017). Only limited information on RO membrane pyrolysis is known so far. Detailed characterization and evaluation of pyrolysis products is required. Besides, char derived from membrane pyrolysis accounts for approx. 20–26 wt% of the module mass (Pontié et al., 2017; Prince et al., 2011), while little attention has been paid to the further study of this product. Considering the large quantities of char produced from membrane modules, it is essential to find a viable application for this product to maximize the utilization of waste membrane materials.

Given the carbon-rich nature, pyrolytic char could serve as a carbon precursor for fabricating carbonaceous materials such as carbon dots (CDs). CDs are an emerging class of nanomaterials broadly employed in analytical detection, biological imaging, drug delivery, photocatalysis, and optoelectronics (Sharma et al., 2017; Song et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021; Zuo et al., 2015), owing to attractive properties such as aqueous dispersibility, photoluminescence (PL) behavior, photostability, easy functionalization, and biocompatibility (Zuo et al., 2016). It is expected that upcycling of char into valuable CDs could derive economic benefits and incentivize waste RO membrane upcycling process. Several studies reported that other types of carbonaceous wastes such as plastic bottles, cups, and bags could be utilized for CDs synthesis and proved the applicability of CDs as fluorescent sensors for metal ions, anions, and bacteria (Chaudhary et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2019b; Kumari and Chaudhary, 2020). Ferric ion (Fe3+) is one of the most essential metal ions in biological systems associated with physiological processes. Both excess and deficiency of Fe3+ may induce physiological disorders, including neurodegenerative diseases, heart dysfunction, anemia, renal failure, and hemochromatosis (Ghule et al., 2016; Jia et al., 2020). Therefore, it is of vital importance to develop a facile and accurate strategy for Fe3+ determination. Recently, CDs have been broadly employed in Fe3+ sensing with high selectivity and sensitivity due to the fluorescence quenching effect (Liu et al., 2017a; Zulfajri et al., 2019). However, the practicability of employing the pyrolytic char derived from waste RO membrane to produce CDs for Fe3+ determination has not been evaluated.

The objective of this work is to investigate the feasibility of upcycling RO membranes into value-added pyrolysis products and subsequent conversion of char into CDs for analytical determination of Fe3+. Using char as carbon precursor, CDs were successfully obtained via hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)-assisted hydrothermal method. This synthetic method is facile and eco-friendly, performed without extra doping agents and complicated post-treatment steps. Different synthesis conditions, including the size of carbon precursor, H2O2 concentration, and reaction time, were investigated to obtain CDs with optimized properties. The CDs were further tested as active materials for sensing Fe3+ ions in different water samples in consequence of fluorescence quenching, and possible mechanism was explored to elucidate the quenching phenomenon.

The waste RO membrane modules were obtained from Separation Technologies Applied Research and Translation (START) center, Singapore. Quinine sulfate, HCl (37%), FeCl3 6H2O, and Ni(NO3)2 6H2O were purchased from Alfa Aesar, US. H2O2 (30 wt%), NaCl, NaOH, and KH2PO4 were obtained from Merck Pte. Ltd, US. H2SO4 (98 wt%), ZnCl2, CuCl2, CaCl2, Ce(NO3)3 6H2O, FeSO4 7H2O, Mg(NO3)2 6H2O, Al(NO3)3 9H2O, Mn(NO3)2 4H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CrCl3 6H2O, and K2HPO4 were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, US. The Milli-Q water used throughout the whole project was obtained from Milli-Q Integral 10 System.

Pyrolysis was carried out in a thermogravimetric apparatus and fixed-bed reactor setup. To observe the thermal behavior of waste RO membrane material, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted using TGA 4000, PerkinElmer. Under N2 atmosphere with a flow rate of 20 mL/min, 10–15 mg of sample was heated at 5 °C/min from 30 °C to 105 °C and maintained for 15 min, followed with heating rate of 10 °C/min to 900 °C and dwell time of 15 min. Then, air was used as purge gas with the same flow rate at 900 °C for another 15 min. The weight loss of sample was recorded as a function of temperature.

The fixed-bed pyrolysis setup for processing of waste membranes is shown in Fig. 1. Fifteen g of the end-of-life RO membrane module (without permeate tube) was first manually cut to obtain small and uniform particles used in the pyrolysis process. Pyrolysis was carried out in a vertical quartz reactor (outer diameter of 4.0 cm). Purging of the setup was conducted using nitrogen (99.9995% purity, Leeden NOX) at 200 mL/min for 10–15 min. After purging, the nitrogen flow rate was changed to 30 mL/min. Meanwhile, the reactor was heated at 10 °C/min to 600 °C and maintained for 30 min at the maximum temperature. Based on preliminary experiments, 30 mL/min was sufficient flow rate for sweeping volatiles from the reactor and maintaining N2 concentration in the collected gas within the calibration range of gas chromatographer for quantitative gas analysis. Gas collection started once the temperature reached 150 °C and finished after dwelling for 30 min at 600 °C. Oil product was collected in two condensers while pyrolytic char was recovered from the pyrolysis reactor. Solidified wax residue was collected from the reactor walls and connecting gas hoses. The mass of solid and liquid products was measured using analytical balance. The volume of each gas component was calculated according to the known volume of nitrogen and non-condensable gas components using a gas chromatograph (GC, Agilent Technologies 7890B). The mass of each gas component was determined under standard temperature and pressure conditions using the following equation:

The sum of all masses of gas components provided the mass of gas produced from the feedstock. The pyrolysis product yields were calculated as the ratios of product masses (i.e., char, oil, gas, and wax residue) to feedstock mass used for pyrolysis experiments. Pyrolysis experiments were conducted in triplicate and pyrolysis product yields were expressed as averages ± standard deviations.

Mortar and pestle milling and mechanical ball milling (Planetary Ball Mills PM 100, Retsch) were used to grind pyrolytic char into fine particles. The pyrolytic char obtained from mortar and pestle milling and ball milling was named Char-A and Char-B, respectively. CDs were synthesized by using the H2O2-assisted hydrothermal route based on the methodology of Hu et al. (2019b). Char (0.25 g) was added into 30 mL aqueous solution containing various H2O2 concentrations. The mixture was placed into Teflon-lined autoclaves (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The produced CDs were naturally cooled down to room temperature and filtered with a 0.22 µm cellulose acetate syringe filter to remove unreacted solid and large nanoparticles. The solution was then dialyzed for three days to remove the remaining impurities, salt, and tiny fragments using dialysis tube (MWCO 3500). The purified powdered CDs were finally obtained by freeze-drying.

Qualitative analysis of pyrolysis oil was conducted using gas chromatography – mass spectrometry (GC-MS, Agilent Technologies 7890B). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7200F) operating at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV was used to observe the morphology and size of pyrolytic char. Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-1400 Plus) was used to view the morphology and size distribution of synthesized CDs under 120 kV. To determine functional groups on the surface of synthesized CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted using an IRPrestige-21 FTIR spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Kratos Analytical AXIS Supra photoelectron spectrometer to obtain the elemental composition of synthesized CDs. CHNS analysis was conducted using Vario EL Cube for the determination of C, H, N, and S content in the membrane, char, and oil. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were recorded on UV-1800 Shimadzu UV-Vis Spectrophotometer. A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer was used for recording photoluminescent spectra of solutions containing CDs. Raman spectrum of CDs was recorded with Horiba Scientific XploRA PLUS Raman Microscope under 532 nm laser excitation. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs was obtained using Bruker D8 Advance equipped with Cu-Kα radiation of 1.54 Å.

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was determined using quinine sulfate as a fluorescence standard reference material (QY= 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4). Both CDs and the reference material were excited at the optimal excitation wavelength of CDs where the strongest emission peak was observed, and the QY was calculated with the following equation (Brouwer, 2011):

where subscripts “CD” and “ref” stand for CDs and reference material, respectively. “Q” denotes to quantum yield (%), “I” refers to the integrated PL intensity, “A” presents the absorbance measured at the optimal excitation wavelength (usually kept below 0.05 to minimize reabsorption effects), and “ η ” refers to the refractive index of solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ in environmental and tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In a total volume system of 2 mL, the initial sensitivity test of CDs (50 μg/mL) to various ions was conducted by investigating PL intensity change after addition of aqueous metal ion solutions (200 μM), including Zn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Ag+, Co2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, and Ni2+ ions. Furthermore, the CDs sensitivity towards Fe3+ was investigated by mixing CDs solution (50 μg/mL) with Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 500 μM. After standing for 20 min, the PL spectrum of mixture excited at 320 nm was obtained, and emission intensity at 450 nm was recorded. All solutions were prepared using Milli-Q water. To further validate the Fe3+ determination in environmental sample, pond water from the Nanyang Technological University campus (1°21′00.0″N 103°40′44.4″E) and tap water from laboratory were used. The pond water was filtered with 0.45 µm cellulose acetate filter membrane and centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 15 min, followed by filtration through 0.22 µm cellulose acetate syringe filter. The Fe3+ content in environmental and tap water samples was determined by Thermo Scientific iCAP Q inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). To measure pH independent response of CDs to Fe3+, both environmental and tap water samples were adjusted to pH 6 (same as Milli-Q water) using 0.1 M HCl solution before the addition of Fe3+. Subsequently, water samples were spiked with known concentrations of Fe3+ (final: 10, 20, and 30 μM), mixed with CDs solution (50 μg/mL), and then analyzed with the abovementioned method. All measurements were taken in triplicate.

The waste RO membrane module used as a feedstock for pyrolysis consisted of a feed spacer (PP), permeate spacer (polyester), permeate carrier (polyethylene terephthalate, PET), and RO membrane. The RO membrane is a thin-film composite material consisting of PA layer on PSU substrate, using non-woven polyester (PET) fabric as a support web, as shown in Fig S1. According to the TGA profile in Fig. S2, a three-stage decomposition of waste RO membrane module was started at approx. 170 °C and completed at approx. 600 °C. Maximum mass loss rates were reached at 320, 420, and 530 °C, respectively, which were mainly attributed to the thermal decomposition of PP, polyester (Pontié et al., 2017), and PSU (Molnár et al., 2005) from the feedstock. Based on these data, 600 °C was the appropriate temperature for pyrolytic decomposition of waste RO membranes in a fixed bed reactor. After fixed bed pyrolysis, char (22 ± 3 wt%), oil (28 ± 4 wt%), gas (17 ± 3 wt%), and wax (18 ± 2 wt%) were collected ( Fig. 2a). The obtained char yield was consistent with the one obtained in TGA apparatus (Fig. S2). The high char content could be attributed to the PET and other polyesters from membrane components which produce large quantities of solid residues during pyrolysis (Williams and Slaney, 2007). The formation of wax residue within the reactor was probably associated with the condensation of long-chain volatile hydrocarbons originated from pyrolysis of PP (Aguado et al., 2002) and the presence of carboxylic acids (e.g., benzoic acid and monovinyl terephthalate) generated from PET degradation (Dhahak et al., 2019).

Fig. 2b displays the qualitative assessment result of organic compounds in the pyrolysis oil from three batches, which were identified using the NIST14 MS library and categorized into seven fractions according to the carbon numbers. Detailed information of identified compounds from one representative oil sample is listed in Table S1. Overall, the pyrolysis oil contained 54.0% gasoline-like (C6-C12), 3.4% diesel-like (C13-C20) hydrocarbon fractions, and 43.1% other hydrocarbon compounds. Among other hydrocarbon compounds, O-containing species were predominant accounting for 21.0%, 11.0%, and 9.0% of O-containing C6-C12, C13-C20, and C21-C22 fractions. The formation of these hydrocarbons could be attributed to decomposition of polymers containing oxygen in their structure, e.g., PET, PSU, and PA. Based on the above characteristics, pyrolysis of RO membranes yields oil with a large fraction of gasoline range hydrocarbons that have great potential to be used as a liquid fuel for transportation purpose. In addition, pyrolysis oil can also serve as a chemical feedstock for monomer recovery and subsequent polymerization (Donaj et al., 2012). However, given the presence of heteroatoms (O, N, and S) in the oil, upgrading of oil (e.g., deoxygenation, denitrification, and desulfurization) is necessary prior to downstream applications.

According to Fig. 2c, the non-condensable gas mainly consisted of methane, ethane, ethylene, propylene, C4 alkenes, C5 alkanes, CO, CO2, and H2. Small quantities of other hydrocarbon compounds such as propane, C4 alkanes, C5 alkanes and alkenes, dienes as well as acetylene were also identified. The large contents of CO (14.6 vol%) and CO2 (44.8 vol%) could be attributed to the presence of PET in the RO membrane module, which serves as the major source of CO and CO2 (Dimitrov et al., 2013; Veksha et al., 2020). Besides, thermal degradation of PA at 600 °C could lead to the generation of CO, CO2, H2, and low share of light hydrocarbons due to the formation of condensed liquids (Arnaiz et al., 2018). H2 (6.1 vol%) and hydrocarbon compounds (34.5 vol%) could also derive from the decomposition of PP, PET, and PSU components in the feedstock (Honus et al., 2018; Veksha et al., 2017). Owing to the presence of H2, CO, and hydrocarbon compounds, non-condensable gas from RO membranes can be utilized as a gaseous fuel for power applications (Sahoo et al., 2021), or chemical feedstock for the production of carbon-based materials (e.g., multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Veksha et al., 2020)) and green chemicals (e.g., methanol (Brigagão et al., 2019)).

The chemical composition of membrane, char, and oil were summarized in Table 1. The N and S content in char may originate from the PA layer and PSU substrate in the membrane structure as well as the inevitable membrane biofouling (Siebdrath et al., 2019). It was also noted the O content underwent a significant reduction from 17.0% of the membrane to 4.1% of the char, indicating the decomposition of O-containing groups during pyrolysis under high temperature (Kaji et al., 1986). These data are consistent with CO and CO2 evolution as shown in Fig. 2c.

To conclude, pyrolysis of waste RO membrane at 600 °C was capable of producing oil with high gasoline-range fractions. However, due to the presence of O-, N-, and S-containing hydrocarbons, further processing of the oil is needed to make it suitable for energy applications. The non-condensable gas consisted of H2, CO, and hydrocarbon compounds also exhibited potential to be utilized as fuel or chemical feedstock. The preliminary results revealed the feasibility and prospect of membrane pyrolysis, thus inspire more efforts to be made on the conversion of such composite membrane waste for economic and environmental benefits.

In this project, H2O2-assisted hydrothermal method was adopted to convert pyrolytic char into fluorescent CDs. In previous reports, hazardous chemicals like corrosive acids (e.g., H2SO4, HNO3 (Kumari et al., 2018)) or organic agents (e.g., CHCl3 (Ramanan et al., 2018)) were used to obtain CDs. H2O2 as a relatively mild but effective oxidant that provides less severe reaction conditions with no toxic gas emissions, making the synthesis process more environmentally friendly and economically viable. Different synthetic conditions were evaluated to obtain CDs with desired physicochemical and optical properties, including the size of carbon precursor, H2O2 concentration, and reaction time. FESEM images in Fig. S3 showed that the pyrolytic char treated with mortar and pestle milling and ball milling possessed average sizes of 138.0 ± 46.5 µm (Char-A) and 11.5 ± 3.7 µm (Char-B), suggesting mechanical ball milling can assist in obtaining smaller and more uniform particles (Loh et al., 2015). Char-A and Char-B particles were used as carbon precursors and heated in varying concentrations of H2O2 at 180 °C for 12 h. Table S2 shows the particle sizes and optical properties (quantum yield, optimal excitation wavelength, and corresponding emission wavelength) of as-prepared CDs. In general, the average particle sizes of all CDs were within the range of 2.6–6.8 nm. However, owing to the incomplete decomposition of bulk carbon structure, large particles (20–65 nm) could also be observed in TEM images, which were excluded from the size measurement of CDs due to their negligible content (< 1%). It is worth noting that more large particles were present in the product derived from Char-A (138 ± 47 µm) when compared with Char-B (12 ± 4 µm), implying carbon precursor with larger particle size is more difficult to convert into CDs. Moreover, the presence of large particles in CDs solution would lead to wide size distribution, which could lower the QY of CDs (Zhao et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 3a, it was found that for CDs derived from Char-B, the highest QY of 6.24% was obtained when using 3% H2O2 solution. For CDs derived from Char-A, the QY was significantly lower (3.25%) when the same concentration of H2O2 was used. Only when the H2O2 concentration increased to 15%, QY of CDs from Char-A reached the maximum (6.83%). It was deduced that in the same environment of 3% H2O2 solution, smaller carbon precursor size would result in the increased surface area, providing a higher possibility for Char-B to access hydroxyl radicals (•OH) generated from H2O2, which is known as one of the most effective oxidizing species. The •OH could break down the CC and C-C bonds in char, resulting in an increased number of periphery carbons which were subsequently oxidized into oxygen-containing groups (Hu et al., 2016). During the hydrothermal reaction, bulk carbon precursors were gradually dissociated into smaller fragments, leading to the formation of CDs. Besides, reaction time also plays a crucial part in CDs production. As shown in Fig. 3b, the QY of CDs gradually increased with the reaction time from 4 to 12 h and then decreased at 16 h. The decreased QY of CDs at excessive reaction time (> 12 h) can be explained by •OH oxidation. It was reported that at prolonged reaction conditions, •OH could attack carbon atoms with hydroxyl and epoxide groups, and eventually destroy CDs (Zhou et al., 2012). Therefore, a balance between different experimental factors was essential for obtaining CDs with maximized PL performance. To conclude, the optimized synthetic condition of CDs was to select Char-B as carbon precursor and heat in the environment of 3% H2O2 solution at 180 °C for 12 h via hydrothermal reaction. Low-concentration H2O2 solution not only ensured a safe reaction environment without danger of explosion but also assisted in producing CDs with strong fluorescence. The as-prepared CDs possessed comparable or higher QY than previously reported CDs derived from non-biodegradable wastes (e.g., plastic bags (Hu et al., 2014), plastic bottles (Hu et al., 2019a), and waste polyolefin residue (Hu et al., 2014)) (Table S3), suggesting their great potential for further applications.

Detailed characterization of CDs synthesized under optimized experimental conditions (Char-B heated at 180 °C for 12 h using 3% H2O2 solution) was conducted. TEM image ( Fig. 4a) of as-prepared CDs exhibited quasi-spherical morphology and good dispersibility in Milli-Q water. Based on measurement of 294 particles, the CDs possessed a narrow size distribution ranging from 1.3 to 6.8 nm and an average size of 3.4 nm (Fig. 4b). Raman spectra (Fig. 4c) displayed two characteristic peaks at 1343 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1, corresponding to disordered (D) carbon structure and graphitic (G) band of sp2 carbon atom (Hu et al., 2019b; Jayaweera et al., 2019b). The ratio of D and G band intensities (ID/IG) was 1.05, indicating the defective structure of synthesized CDs. As shown in Fig. 4d, the XRD pattern of resulting CDs displayed three diffraction peaks centered at around 12.4°, 25.3°, and 41.0°, respectively. According to Bragg’s Law, the calculated interlayer spacing of peaks at 25.3° and 41.0° was 0.35 nm and 0.22 nm, which is consistent with the (002) and (100) lattice spacing of graphitic carbon, respectively (JCPDS file No. 75–1621) (Roy et al., 2014). The peak located at 12.4° was shifted compared to the peaks corresponding to graphite crystal phase (002), suggesting the increased interlayer spacing probably due to the introduction of oxygen-containing groups and presence of water molecules (Shalaby et al., 2015; Siburian et al., 2018). In general, the overall broad peak shape from XRD pattern demonstrated the low crystallinity and high degree of disorder of synthesized CDs.

FTIR spectrum was used to characterize the functional groups on CDs surface. As shown in Fig. 5a, the typical absorption bands located at 3201 and 3046 cm−1 correspond to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 1717 cm−1, 1686 cm−1, and 1630 cm−1 are attributed to the CO, CN, and CC bond, respectively (Jayaweera et al., 2019b). The small peak at 1211 cm−1 is assigned to C-N/C-S bond (Ding et al., 2014). The strong absorption peaks at 1402 cm−1 and 1035 cm−1 correspond to C-H bending vibration and C-O stretching vibration. The above results demonstrated the presence of abundant oxygen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups, which were responsible for the excellent dispersibility of CDs in aqueous solution. Besides, the peak of O-H showed stronger intensity than that of CO, suggesting the number of hydroxyl groups on CDs surface was higher than carboxyl and carbonyl groups. It has been reported that hydroxyl groups as electron donor can effectively enhance fluorescence intensity (Wang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2016b), while carboxyl and carbonyl groups with strong electron-withdrawing ability would weaken fluorescence emission via the formation of non-radiative recombination centers (Liu et al., 2011; Zheng et al., 2011). Therefore, it is possible that the presence of abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface significantly contributed to the PL behavior of CDs.

XPS analysis is an alternative approach to characterize the surface state and composition of CDs. Fig. 5b showed the peaks corresponding to binding energies of C1s (283 eV), O1s (530 eV), N1s (398 eV), S2p (165 eV), and S2s (229 eV). Based on the integrated peak area, the atomic ratios of these four elements were 56.9%, 36.5%, 4.8%, 1.8%, respectively. Besides, unmarked peaks in Fig. 5b at 26, 99, and 149 eV were assigned to Ca3p, Si 2p, and Si2s from the background instead of sample itself (Yang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2016a). The high O content confirmed the presence of abundant oxygen functional groups on CDs surface. Also, the presence of N and S elements confirmed the successful doping of CDs with two other heteroatoms. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) could be resolved into three peaks of C-C/CC at 284.8 eV, C-O/C-N/C-S at 286.2 eV, and CO at 288.8 eV, respectively (Hu et al., 2019a; Jayaweera et al., 2019a; Xiao et al., 2016). Based on the relative area of the peaks in C1s spectrum, the content of CO was significantly lower than that of C-O/C-N/C-S. As the N and S only shared a small proportion when compared with the O, the high content of C-O/C-N/C-S could be mainly attributed to the presence of abundant hydroxyl groups. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 5d) showed peaks of C-O and CO at 530.7 and 531.8 eV (Wei et al., 2014), further confirmed the introduction of oxygen-containing groups. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 5e) was deconvoluted into two peaks of C-N at 398.14 eV and N-CO at 400.10 eV (Kim et al., 2012; Lu et al., 2016). The S2p spectrum (Fig. 5f) was deconvoluted into two peaks of C-S-C2p2/3 at 166.25 eV and C-S-C2p1/2 at 167.31 eV (Liang et al., 2012). Consistent with FTIR analysis, the XPS results also proved the presence of oxygenated functional groups and successful doping of N and S, which could generate various surface defects and act as capture centers for excitons, providing a synergistic effect on the PL behavior of CDs (Yan et al., 2019).

UV-Vis and PL spectroscopies were applied to investigate the optical properties of CDs. As shown in Fig. 6a, the UV-Vis spectrum of CDs exhibited a broad peak between 290 and 310 nm, which was attributed to n-π * electron transition of CO bond (Zhu et al., 2015). Under 365 nm UV irradiation, strong blue fluorescence of CDs was observed (Fig. 6a, inset). The corresponding emission peaks of PL spectra red shifted from 450 to 477 nm with the increase in excitation wavelength from 310 to 400 nm. The maximum emission peak was observed at 450 nm upon excitation at 320 nm. The excitation-dependent PL behavior and strong fluorescence may be associated with the synergistic effect of varying particle sizes and diverse surface defect states generated by surface oxidation and heteroatom doping (Sun et al., 2006).

To evaluate the PL stability of CDs, the influence of various environmental conditions was also investigated, including storage time, ionic strength, pH, and temperature. As shown in Fig. 7a, the CDs demonstrated excellent stability within the storage time of 30 days. Also, the CDs exhibited good stability against varying ionic strength with NaCl concentrations between 0 and 1 M, suggesting their resistance to harsh environment with high ionic strength. Fig. 7b shows that the PL intensity of CDs remained stable at pH 4–6 but slightly increased at pH 7–9, which could be ascribed to the deprotonation of surface groups in alkaline solution (Devi et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2017b). The PL intensity exhibited a slight decrease at lower pH (pH<4) and higher pH (pH>9), which may be due to the aggregation of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups via hydrogen bonding at extreme pH range (Guo et al., 2020). When the solution temperature was increased from 10 to 60 °C, the CDs displayed the highest PL intensity at room temperature, while lower and higher temperatures both led to a slight decrease in PL intensity. However, this was reversible when the solution temperature returned to room temperature, suggesting temperature variation in this work didn’t have a destructive effect on CDs properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ in environmental and tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of CDs towards various ions was studied, these included: Zn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Ag+, Co2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, and Ni2+. In this experiment, metal ions at a concentration of 200 μM were individually mixed with CDs solution (50 μg/mL). As shown in Fig. 8a, Fe3+ exhibited a significantly higher quenching efficiency (79%) compared to other metal ions, indicating the high sensitivity of produced CDs towards Fe3+. Then, decreased PL intensity was observed by mixing CDs solutions (50 μg/mL) with Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 500 μM. The analytical relationship between (I-I0)/I0 and Fe3+ concentrations was presented in Fig. 8b-c. Besides, the inset of Fig. 8c displayed a linear relationship within the range of 0–100 μM (R2 = 0.998). According to the 3δ/s rule (where δ stands for the intensity standard deviation of ten blank measurements and s is the slope of the calibration curve) (Xu et al., 2019), the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 2.97 μM. This value was lower than the maximum level of Fe3+ permitted in drinking water (0.3 mg/L or 5.4 μM) according to U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (Zhang et al., 2020), allowing CDs to be employed for evaluating the quality of environmental and tap water samples in terms of allowed Fe3+ concentrations. Table S4 displayed the comparison of other CDs used for Fe3+ determination, including linear detection range and LOD. The results demonstrated the comparable or even better capability of CDs developed from waste RO membrane modules for Fe3+ determination with a relatively wide detection range and low LOD.

According to previous studies, Fe3+ usually exhibited stronger binding affinity to surface functional groups of CDs, such as oxygen-containing groups, amino and amide groups (Liu et al., 2015). To explore possible quenching mechanism, several experiments were carried out. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra (Fig. 8d) were obtained to determine the average PL lifetime of CDs with and without the presence of Fe3+, which was calculated to be 2.93 ns and 2.72 ns, respectively. The nearly unchanged PL lifetime implied the quenching mechanism of CDs could be described as static quenching induced by the formation of nonfluorescent ground-state complex (Zu et al., 2017). Moreover, Fig. S4 showed that after the addition of Fe3+, the absorption spectrum did not fit well with the theoretical curve (physical addition of the spectra of CDs and Fe3+ solution), further indicating the formation of CDs-Fe3+ complex system due to coordinated interaction between Fe3+ and surface functional groups of CDs. Besides, brown aggregation of CDs-Fe3+ complex system could be visually observed in the presence of Fe3+ for 1 h (Fig. S5a). TEM images also confirmed the change of CDs from monodispersed particles to aggregated complexes (20–50 nm) after the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. S5b-c). However, it was deduced that inner filter effect may also contribute to the quenching process. According to Fig. S4, the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ partially overlapped with the excitation spectrum of CDs, which could attenuate the excitation light to a certain degree (Zu et al., 2017).

Finally, the applicability of CDs for Fe3+ determination was validated by using environmental and tap water samples. To minimize possible experimental error, both water samples were adjusted to pH 6, which was similar to the pH of Milli-Q water used for preparing CDs and metal ion solution. According to ICP-MS measurement, the Fe3+ content found in environmental and tap water samples was 0.11 μM and 0.07 μM, respectively. No Fe3+ in both water samples were detected by CDs as the Fe3+ concentrations were lower than the LOD of CDs. After spiking for 20 min with Fe3+ solution of different concentrations (10, 20, and 30 μM), the PL intensity was recorded. The corresponding concentration, recovery, and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated and shown in Table 2. The recovery of Fe3+ ranged between 92.6% and 103.1%, and the RSD was from 0.29% to 3.22%, indicating the feasibility of CDs to be used as a fluorescent sensor in real water systems for Fe3+ determination.

An economically viable and environmentally friendly strategy for upcycling exhausted RO membrane modules, a type of non-biodegradable plastic waste, was developed via thermochemical conversion followed by H2O2-assisted hydrothermal method. The modules were pyrolyzed at 600 °C to produce oil (28 wt%), non-condensable gas (18 wt%), and char (22 wt%). Based on characteristics, oil and gas were promising sources of fuel and chemical feedstock. On the other hand, char was found as a suitable carbon precursor for N, S co-doped CDs synthesis as it contained high carbon content and heteroatoms originated from RO membrane components. Compared with mortar and pestle milling, mechanical ball milling could grind char into more uniform and smaller particles (Char-B), which were beneficial for producing CDs with higher QY at relatively low H2O2 concentrations. The optimized synthesis of CDs from Char-B with the highest QY was achieved using 3% H2O2 solution at 180 °C for 12 h. The resulting CDs displayed excellent water dispersibility and stable optical properties. It was found the CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+ as a synergistic action of static quenching and inner filter effect. This study demonstrates the feasibility of upcycling waste RO membrane modules into pyrolysis products as potential fuel and chemical feedstock, and CDs for Fe3+ determination, providing an alternative strategy of managing this kind of waste in a sustainable manner.

Shane Allen Snyder: Validation. Grzegorz Lisak: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.
